collections.name: Earth & Climate
collections.desc: Our changing planet, watched from orbit.

collections.name: Remote Sensing
collections.desc: How a satellite turns light into data.

collections.name: Satellites & Orbits
collections.desc: The machines and paths of observation.

collections.name: The Sun & Space Weather
collections.desc: Our star, and the storms it sends.

collections.name: The Solar System
collections.desc: Worlds, moons, and the dance of orbits.

collections.name: Stars, Galaxies & the Cosmos
collections.desc: From nearby stars to the deep field.

collections.name: Mission & Data Tools
collections.desc: The SSAI lens — observation to understanding.

modules.title: Rotating Earth
modules.blurb: A photoreal hero globe of the real NASA Blue Marble, slowly auto-rotating with atmospheric rim glow and day-side terminator shading, marked with NASA science and mission sites.
modules.interaction: Auto-rotates west-to-east on the real Blue Marble; drag (mouse or touch) to spin and tilt the globe with eased momentum, hover a glowing marker to reveal its site label, and the readout tracks the lat/lon at the globe's center.

modules.title: Earth data layers
modules.credit: Data · NASA Blue Marble / Black Marble (MODIS · VIIRS)
modules.blurb: Flagship hero: a bright, cinematic auto-rotating real Blue Marble globe that swaps between NASA satellite data layers (true color, night lights, vegetation, sea-surface temperature, aerosol) to demonstrate SSAI's 48 years of Earth-science data fluency.
modules.interaction: Drag the globe to spin it (with momentum); release to resume the slow auto-rotation. Tap a translucent chip along the bottom to switch the active NASA data layer; the corner HUD updates with the layer name, a one-line description, and the data-source credit.

modules.title: Day & Night
modules.credit: Data · NASA Blue Marble / Black Marble
modules.blurb: Shows Earth's real day/night terminator with NASA Blue Marble daylight and Black Marble city lights, swept by time-of-day and tilted by season.
modules.interaction: Drag the time-of-day slider to rotate the terminator and move the subsolar point; drag the season slider to tilt it toward polar day/night; press play to auto-advance time.

modules.title: Aurora forecast
modules.blurb: Lets viewers drag the NOAA Kp index (0-9) and watch the real auroral oval expand to lower latitudes over a north-polar view of Earth, showing where the aurora would be visible tonight.
modules.interaction: Drag the Kp slider (or use arrow keys) to set planetary geomagnetic activity; the glowing auroral oval expands equatorward and intensifies, the readout updates the equatorward boundary latitude and visible regions, and a tap on the visual toggles the day/night terminator base map.

modules.title: Data flow-field
modules.blurb: A mesmerizing particle flow-field that advects ~1500 points along a smooth pseudo-noise vector field — turning Earth-observation data into living art, like ocean currents and atmospheric flow.
modules.interaction: Move the pointer (or touch) to inject a gentle swirling current the particles bend around; otherwise it flows on its own. Honors prefers-reduced-motion by rendering a single static frame.

modules.title: Storm Tracker
modules.blurb: A live hurricane-tracking console rendering a recurving Atlantic storm — rotating spiral, clear eye, Saffir-Simpson category, past track and forecast cone — over a real NASA satellite view of the Atlantic Ocean.
modules.interaction: Press Play to fly the storm along its recurving track as it intensifies over warm water and weakens over land, or drag the timeline to scrub to any point; the category badge, max wind, central pressure, coordinates, track and forecast cone all update live.

modules.title: Thermal fire detection
modules.blurb: Runs a satellite active-fire detector across a real NASA MODIS scene of the 2021 Dixie Fire: a scan line sweeps the true-color image and ignites burn-scar hotspots wherever the actual shortwave-infrared band is high and vegetation index is low.
modules.interaction: Loads the real true-color MODIS scene and renders immediately; a scan line sweeps left-to-right and flags burn-scar / active-fire hotspots by reading the genuine SWIR (2130 nm) and NDVI bands from the image, lighting them as pulsing detections with a running count, swath-progress and peak-SWIR readout. Toggle Optical vs Thermal SWIR to recolor the same real pixels as a heat ramp and see why fire pops in shortwave-infrared; hover the scene for a live latitude/longitude readout.

modules.title: Rising Tide — Nile Delta
modules.blurb: Visualizes how progressive sea-level rise inundates a real low-lying coastline. The Nile Delta (NASA MODIS true-color) fills the frame; a translucent, shimmering water layer floods the lowest terrain first as you raise sea level, with live readouts for rise (m), land inundated (%), and a population-at-risk proxy.
modules.interaction: Drag the sea-level slider to raise the water line from 0 to 10 m. Darker (lower) terrain floods first using image brightness as an elevation proxy; the shoreline breathes and the flooded water caustically shimmers. At rest, ambient tide animation and drifting light keep the scene alive.

modules.title: The CO₂ record
modules.blurb: A cinematic, data-credible rendering of the real NOAA Mauna Loa annual-mean atmospheric CO₂ record (1959–2025), the Keeling Curve. Use it as a flagship hero visualization in SSAI's brand book to demonstrate the company's NASA-grade Earth-science heritage — a single, authoritative chart that animates the rise from 315.98 ppm to 427.35 ppm against a pre-industrial baseline, with a live hover/scrub readout for exploring any year. Suited to web, presentations, and investor or capability decks where scientific authority and visual prestige both matter.
modules.interaction: On load, the line draws on left-to-right with an accent-colored stroke and a soft area glow beneath it, then a focus dot settles on the latest year (2025). Move the pointer (or finger) anywhere over the chart to scrub: a vertical guide, a focus dot, and a floating readout snap to the nearest year and show the real ppm value plus the cumulative rise since 1959. Move away and it relaxes back to the latest reading. A dashed pre-industrial baseline (~280 ppm) is annotated, and two stat callouts report the total rise since 1959 (~+35%) and versus pre-industrial (~+50%). Animation pauses when scrolled off-screen, respects prefers-reduced-motion (renders the final frame instantly), and re-fits crisply on resize at any container size.

modules.title: Atmospheric profile
modules.blurb: Explore the real vertical structure of Earth's atmosphere (temperature, pressure, density) from sea level to 100 km using the US Standard Atmosphere 1976.
modules.interaction: Drag the glowing altitude marker up and down the profile (or click anywhere on the chart) to read real temperature, pressure and density at any height; the active atmospheric layer is highlighted and labeled. Toggle the secondary curve between pressure and density.

modules.title: Earth Explorer — live NASA map
modules.blurb: A real GIS-style slippy map of LIVE NASA satellite imagery — pan and zoom into true-colour and false-colour MODIS, sea-surface temperature and aerosols, stepping day by day, exactly like NASA Worldview.
modules.interaction: Drag to pan, scroll or use +/− to zoom into live NASA EOSDIS GIBS tiles; switch layers (True Color, Bands 7·2·1, Sea-Surface Temp, Aerosols), step the date, and read the live latitude/longitude under your cursor.

modules.title: Band Combiner
modules.blurb: Switch the same LIVE NASA scene between band combinations — natural colour, vegetation/fire false colour, and snow/ice — and zoom in anywhere on Earth to see how mapping different wavelengths to red/green/blue reveals different things.
modules.interaction: Drag to pan and scroll to zoom into live MODIS imagery; switch the band recipe (Natural / Vegetation & fire / Snow & ice) to remap the wavelengths to R·G·B and watch fires, vegetation and ice pop; step the date day by day.

modules.title: Live NDVI
modules.blurb: A LIVE, zoomable vegetation-greenness map: NDVI computed in your browser from real MODIS near-infrared and red bands, so dense vegetation reads green and bare ground brown — anywhere on Earth, any day.
modules.interaction: Drag to pan, scroll to zoom into live MODIS imagery recoloured by NDVI = (NIR−Red)/(NIR+Red); step the date day by day to watch vegetation green up and senesce. The legend maps colour to NDVI value.

modules.title: Pixel Drill-Down
modules.blurb: Click ANY point on a live, zoomable NASA map and read that pixel's real MODIS reflectance — Red, Green, Blue, NIR and SWIR — plus the computed NDVI and a surface classification.
modules.interaction: Pan and zoom the live MODIS map, then click/tap a point to drop a pin and sample the genuine satellite reflectance at that location: the five bands, NDVI = (NIR−Red)/(NIR+Red), and a land-cover guess. Step the date to compare days.

modules.title: Resolution
modules.blurb: An interactive ground-sample-distance explorer that block-averages real NASA MODIS imagery so detail emerges as the sample shrinks across sensor classes.
modules.interaction: Drag the GSD slider or tap a sensor preset (MODIS 250m to Commercial 0.5m); the scene re-samples in real time, the GSD readout and sample count update, and the feature legend lights up each detail as it becomes resolvable.

modules.title: Spectral Signatures
modules.blurb: Every surface on Earth reflects sunlight in a unique way across the electromagnetic spectrum — a fingerprint that lets remote sensing instruments tell vegetation from water, soil from snow, nature from the built world. This instrument plots reflectance against wavelength from the visible through near- and short-wave infrared, revealing why a healthy leaf glows in the near-infrared while water vanishes, and how snow betrays itself by collapsing in the SWIR.
modules.interaction: Drag the wavelength marker anywhere across the spectrum to read each material's exact reflectance at that band; the corner readout updates live. Toggle materials on and off from the legend to isolate or compare signatures. The curves breathe and shimmer at rest, with the visible / NIR / SWIR bands softly shaded behind them.

modules.title: Radar vs. Optical
modules.credit: Optical · NASA MODIS   ·   SAR · ESA Copernicus Sentinel-1 (CC BY-SA 4.0)
modules.blurb: Demonstrates why Earth-observation missions fly both sensors: as cloud cover rises it blinds the optical view while the radar image stays perfectly crisp.
modules.interaction: Drag the CLOUD COVER slider — drifting cloud rolls in and whites out the optical (left) half while the SAR (right) half stays sharp.

modules.title: Spectrum explorer
modules.blurb: An interactive electromagnetic-spectrum explainer: drag a marker across the bands to learn each one's wavelength range and what it lets scientists observe.
modules.interaction: Drag the marker (or click/tap anywhere on the spectrum bar, or use arrow keys when focused) to move across the seven bands; the wave, accent colour and info panel update live to the selected band.

modules.title: Satellite ground tracks
modules.blurb: Watch real LEO satellites — Landsat 9, Suomi-NPP, ICESat-2 and Aqua — trace sinusoidal ground tracks that drift westward across NASA Blue Marble imagery with a live day/night terminator.
modules.interaction: Hover or tap a track (or its legend chip) to highlight a satellite and read altitude, inclination and period; play/pause, scrub time-speed, and lengthen the orbital trails over the real Earth.

modules.title: Families of Earth Orbit
modules.blurb: A cinematic, full-bleed visualization of the principal Earth-orbit families — LEO, MEO, GEO, Molniya, and Polar / Sun-synchronous — rendered simultaneously around a large Blue Marble, each with a labelled satellite moving at the correct relative speed. Viewers highlight a family to read its altitude band, period, inclination, and mission role, illustrating how altitude, shape and inclination define what an orbit is for.
modules.interaction: Alive at rest: all five orbits animate continuously with satellites tracing their paths at physically faithful relative speeds, the Earth slowly rotating its glow, and a drifting starfield. Click any legend entry (or its satellite) to highlight that family — the chosen orbit and satellite brighten while the others dim, and HUD chips update to show its altitude band, orbital period, inclination, and typical use. Click again or pick another to change focus. Everything self-heals on resize.

modules.title: Orbit Designer
modules.blurb: Lets you shape a real satellite orbit around a Blue-Marble Earth and read its geometry — apogee, perigee, period, inclination and orbit class — updating live as you drag.
modules.interaction: Drag the three sliders (apogee, perigee, inclination) to reshape the glowing orbit; the satellite always travels at true Kepler speed and the corner chips recompute period and orbit class instantly.

modules.title: Swath Scanner
modules.blurb: Shows how a polar-orbiting satellite sweeps an imaging swath along an inclined ground track and accumulates global coverage pass by pass as the Earth rotates beneath it.
modules.interaction: Drag the single slider to widen or narrow the imaging swath; the painted coverage band, revisit time, and coverage percentage respond live as the satellite scans continuously.

modules.title: Build a constellation
modules.blurb: Lets a prospect design a LEO imaging constellation on the real NASA Blue Marble and instantly see how fleet size, altitude and inclination drive global daily coverage and revisit.
modules.interaction: Drag three sliders -- satellites (1-40), altitude (400-1200 km) and inclination (0-98 deg, or tap an SSO/Polar/Mid/Equatorial preset) -- and circular ground footprints tile across the real Earth while global coverage %, average revisit and passes/day update live; Reset restores the default fleet.

modules.title: Data downlink
modules.blurb: Visualizes the journey of Earth-observation data from satellite acquisition through downlink, ground processing, and final data product, with a live end-to-end latency budget.
modules.interaction: Drag the downlink-rate and pass-geometry sliders to retime the pipeline, toggle play/pause and a fault injector, and hover any stage to reveal what it does while packets flow and the latency clock recomputes.

modules.title: Orbital debris
modules.blurb: Visualize how rising space debris drives the Kessler cascade around Earth's LEO shell and GEO ring.
modules.interaction: Drag the debris-density slider to thicken the cloud and trigger self-spawning collision flashes; toggle Classify to color active satellites vs debris; Reset clears the field.

modules.title: Orbit Tracker
modules.blurb: Live ground-track visualizer plotting real NASA/ESA Earth-observation missions over a true-color Blue Marble world map, using their actual orbital altitude, inclination and period.
modules.interaction: Hover or tap a satellite dot or its label to reveal altitude, inclination and period; use the small overlaid HUD chips to play/pause and change time-warp speed, and toggle the night-lights layer.

modules.title: Atmospheric Reentry Heating
modules.blurb: Visualizes the searing plasma-shock heating a crewed capsule endures during hypersonic atmospheric reentry, from exo-atmospheric interface down through peak heating to the glowing limb of Earth — the discipline behind thermal-protection-system engineering and entry, descent and landing analysis.
modules.interaction: A single slider sets the entry speed and angle (gentle skip to steep ballistic). As it changes, the plasma shock-sheath brightness, ablation ember trail, peak heat flux, plasma temperature and G-load all respond. The descent auto-loops continuously, alive at rest. Corner HUD chips read altitude, velocity in Mach, G-load, peak heat flux and plasma temperature in real time.

modules.title: The Living Sun
modules.blurb: A cinematic, living solar disk built on real NASA SDO imagery with a wavelength switcher (171Å gold corona / cooler teal channel), slow rotation, breathing coronal glow, and an atmospheric halo — used as the hero "real science" centerpiece in the SSAI brand book.
modules.interaction: Click the wavelength chips (or press 1 / 2, or use arrow keys) to switch between the gold 171Å and cooler teal SDO channels with a cross-dissolve; the disk slowly rotates, the corona breathes, and a live caption ticks — hover the disk to pause rotation and reveal a focus reticle.

modules.title: Space Weather
modules.blurb: Shows the live Sun-to-Earth connection — solar wind, magnetosphere response, auroral oval and the real NOAA G-scale / Kp index — so viewers feel how solar activity drives geomagnetic storms.
modules.interaction: Drag the single slider to raise or lower solar-wind speed; the wind stream, auroral oval, Kp readout and NOAA G-scale all respond, and the scene keeps breathing on its own at rest.

modules.title: Coronal Mass Ejection
modules.blurb: Shows a coronal mass ejection erupting from the Sun and propagating across interplanetary space to compress Earth's magnetosphere and ignite a geomagnetic storm.
modules.interaction: Drag the SHOCK SPEED slider to set the plasma front's velocity (which changes the Earth-arrival ETA), or press ERUPT to launch a CME on demand — it also auto-loops on its own.

modules.title: Earth's Magnetosphere
modules.blurb: Visualizes how Earth's dipole magnetic field deflects the solar wind — a compressed sunward bow shock and magnetopause, a streaming magnetotail, charged particles flowing in and deflecting around the shield, and auroral ovals that brighten as solar-wind pressure rises. Drag the pressure slider to watch the standoff distance shrink, the field compress, and the aurora intensify in real time.
modules.interaction: Continuous animation runs on load: solar-wind particles stream from the sunward edge and deflect around the magnetopause, field lines shimmer, the magnetotail flutters, and auroral ovals pulse. One slider sets solar-wind dynamic pressure; raising it compresses the dayside, pushes the standoff distance inward, speeds the wind, and intensifies the aurora and bow-shock glow. HUD chips report solar-wind speed, Bz, Kp index, and magnetopause standoff distance.

modules.title: Planet Viewer
modules.blurb: A cinematic planetarium-grade viewer that lets an audience explore the Solar System on real NASA/JPL/USGS full-disk imagery. Drop it into the SSAI brand experience (web, pitch decks, kiosk) to demonstrate, in a single credible interaction, the company's 48 years of planetary and Earth science: a viewer selects any body from Mercury to Neptune (plus the Moon), watches it crossfade in with a soft atmospheric glow and slow float, and reads accurate physical data — diameter, day length, year, moons, temperature and distance from the Sun. Earth is rendered as a true rotating globe from the Blue Marble equirectangular texture; the photographic disks float with a subtle parallax. Works at any pop-up size, is devicePixelRatio-aware, pauses off-screen and respects reduced-motion.
modules.interaction: Click a planet dot or name in the selector rail to switch bodies; the new world cinematically crossfades over the old one while the fact panel cross-updates. Earth auto-rotates as a real globe; all bodies have a slow cinematic float. Hover a selector item to preview its name. Keyboard: focus the rail and use Left/Right arrows (or Up/Down) to step between worlds; Home/End jump to the first/last. Everything renders a first frame on init with no gating; animation pauses when scrolled off-screen and falls back to a static, fully-rendered frame under prefers-reduced-motion.

modules.title: Moon phase
modules.blurb: An interactive lunar phase explorer: a procedurally crater-textured Moon whose terminator shadow sweeps from new to full as you drag the disc or move the slider, with live phase name and illuminated percentage.
modules.interaction: Drag the Moon left/right (or move the slider) to scrub the full synodic cycle; tap New / First quarter / Full / Last quarter chips for a smooth eased jump. The terminator, phase name, and illumination readout update live.

modules.title: The Orrery
modules.blurb: A cinematic, jewel-like model of the solar system in motion — the Sun ablaze at centre with eight planets sweeping their luminous orbits at true relative speeds, distances log-compressed so Mercury through Neptune fill a single frame. Hover or tap any planet to read its name, orbital period and distance from the Sun; drag the speed dial to slow the dance to a meditative crawl or spin a century in seconds.
modules.interaction: Continuously alive on load — planets orbit automatically. Hover (or tap on touch) any planet or its orbit to surface a HUD card with the planet's name, orbital period and distance in AU; the selected world brightens with a halo. A single speed slider, overlaid bottom-left, scales time from a near-still drift to a brisk century-per-minute sweep. All controls fade with the global hide-controls toggle.

modules.title: Total Solar Eclipse — Geometry of Totality
modules.blurb: Reveals the precise geometry of a solar eclipse: the Moon's disc tracking across a radiant Sun until totality unveils the pearly streaming corona, the diamond-ring flash and Baily's beads, while the sky darkens to deep indigo and stars emerge.
modules.interaction: Drag the slider to move the Moon across the Sun, or let it auto-play through a full eclipse cycle. HUD chips report phase, percent obscured and magnitude; a small inset diagram shows the Sun–Moon–Earth alignment with umbra and penumbra cones.

modules.title: Comet — Coma & Anti-Sunward Tails
modules.blurb: Shows how a comet brightens, grows a glowing coma, and unfurls its ion and dust tails as it approaches perihelion — with both tails always streaming directly away from the Sun.
modules.interaction: Drag the orbit slider to scrub the comet from aphelion to perihelion and back, or let it auto-orbit. As it nears the Sun the coma swells, the warm golden dust tail curves and the blue ion tail lengthens — both anti-sunward. HUD chips read out heliocentric distance (AU), orbital speed (km/s) and tail length.

modules.title: The Five Lagrange Points
modules.blurb: Reveals the five Sun-Earth Lagrange points where gravitational and orbital forces balance, rendered over a living gravitational-potential field. L1 and L2 host SSAI-supported missions SOHO and JWST, shown in gentle halo orbits as the whole system co-rotates. Click any point to read its stability, distance from Earth, and resident mission.
modules.interaction: The Sun, Earth and all five Lagrange points sit on a slowly co-rotating orbital plane over a glowing saddle-contour potential field that fills the frame; SOHO at L1 and JWST at L2 trace small halo-orbit wobbles. Hover or click any Lagrange point to select it — a HUD chip reports its name, stability, distance from Earth and resident mission. A single slider adjusts the field intensity, and a toggle shows/hides the contour field.

modules.title: Two Tides a Day
modules.blurb: Reveals why Earth has two tidal bulges and two high tides daily, with the Moon's gravity stretching the ocean on both the near and far sides, and the Sun's alignment driving spring and neap tides.
modules.interaction: Alive on load: the Moon orbits Earth, Earth rotates, and the two ocean bulges track the Moon while a surface marker traces a two-peak daily tide curve. A single slider sweeps the Moon-Sun alignment from spring (aligned) to neap (90 degrees), reshaping the bulges in real time. HUD chips report lunar angle, marker tide height, and spring/neap state.

modules.title: Goldilocks Zone
modules.blurb: Shows why a star's temperature and luminosity set where liquid water — and life — can exist.
modules.interaction: Drag the star's temperature slider to reshape the green habitable ring, then drag the planet in or out to see it turn scorched, ocean-blue, or ice-white with a live verdict.

modules.title: Starfield — Yale Bright Star Catalogue night sky
modules.blurb: An immersive full-bleed night sky rendered from the real brightest stars (RA/Dec, apparent magnitude, B-V spectral color, distance) with Orion and Ursa Major drawn from true positions, gentle drift and twinkle, and hover readouts.
modules.interaction: Move the pointer over any bright star to reveal its real name, apparent magnitude, spectral type and distance; drag horizontally to pan across the sky; toggle the constellation-lines and labels chips in the HUD; the sky drifts and twinkles on its own and pauses when off-screen.

modules.title: Deep-Field Explorer
modules.credit: Image · NASA, ESA, CSA, STScI — JWST (SMACS 0723)
modules.blurb: An immersive pan-and-zoom explorer built on JWST's real first deep field (SMACS 0723) that lets viewers dive into thousands of real galaxies and annotated lensing features.
modules.interaction: Drag to pan and scroll, pinch, or use the +/- buttons to zoom deep into the real galaxies; hover or tap to reveal elegant annotation pins on lensed arcs, ancient galaxies and the foreground cluster, with a live scale/redshift readout.

modules.title: Nebula Viewer — JWST
modules.credit: Image · NASA, ESA, CSA, STScI — JWST
modules.blurb: An observatory-grade viewer for switching, panning, zooming, and annotating real JWST nebula imagery (Cosmic Cliffs & Pillars of Creation).
modules.interaction: Drag to pan and scroll/pinch or the +/- controls to zoom into the real nebula; click the named tabs to crossfade between Cosmic Cliffs and Pillars of Creation; hover or tap the glowing pins to reveal one-line facts about each star-forming feature.

modules.title: Anatomy of a Spiral Galaxy
modules.blurb: An interactive, scientifically grounded model of spiral-galaxy structure for SSAI's Instrument Library. It renders a face-on disk galaxy from its real components — a bright central bulge of old stars, a thin stellar disk, logarithmic spiral arms traced by blue young O/B stars and pink HII star-forming regions, a dark dust lane, and a diffuse halo — all set in differential rotation (inner stars orbit faster than outer ones, the physical origin of how arms wind). Viewers adjust the arm pitch angle (loose vs. tightly wound, the property astronomers use to classify Sa–Sc spirals) and isolate any single component to read a one-line description of what it is. It replaces the previous "generator" toy with a quiet, museum-grade explanation of galaxy anatomy.
modules.interaction: Loads with the galaxy already drawn and slowly rotating with correct differential motion (the synchronous first frame is a fully formed galaxy, never a black void). A single slider sets the spiral arm pitch angle from ~10 deg (tightly wound, Sa-type) to ~30 deg (loose, open, Sc-type); dragging it re-shapes the logarithmic arms live. A small row of pills lets the viewer highlight one component at a time — Bulge, Disk, Arms, HII regions, or Halo — which dims the rest, draws a soft emphasis, and reveals a one-line scientific caption for that structure; "All" restores the full luminous view. Corner chips quietly report the current pitch angle and morphological type. Resizing the frame re-derives all dimensions so the galaxy always fills the canvas edge to edge.

modules.title: Relativistic Sky
modules.blurb: A grounded demonstration of how the sky would actually look from a ship moving near the speed of light. As you raise the velocity (β = v/c), the real special-relativity optics take hold: stars crowd toward your direction of travel (relativistic aberration), the field ahead blue-shifts while the field behind red-shifts (relativistic Doppler), and the forward cone brightens through relativistic beaming (the "headlight" effect). Useful for teaching SR optics with correct numbers — Lorentz factor, aberration angle, and Doppler factor are all reported live.
modules.interaction: One control: a velocity slider for β = v/c, from 0 to 0.99. Drag it up and the dense starfield migrates forward, tints blue ahead and red behind, and the forward hemisphere brightens — exactly per the aberration and Doppler equations. Three quiet corner chips report β, the Lorentz factor γ, and the forward aberration half-angle. The view holds a gentle continuous drift so the sky feels alive even when you leave the slider untouched.

modules.title: Pulsar — a cosmic lighthouse
modules.blurb: A grounded model of a rotating neutron star whose two opposed magnetic-axis radio beams sweep across space. The spin axis and the magnetic axis are offset, so as the star rotates the beams trace cones on the sky; each time a beam crosses Earth's line of sight, the observer records a sharp pulse. A live folded pulse profile builds up exactly as a radio astronomer would record it, spiking once per beam pass. Choose among real pulsars — the Crab (33 ms, young, strong field), Vela (89 ms), and a recycled millisecond pulsar (1.4 ms, ancient, weak field) — to see how spin period and magnetic field together define a neutron star's life stage.
modules.interaction: Drag the spin-period slider to set how fast the neutron star rotates — the beams sweep faster and the folded profile ticks at the new rate. Tap a named preset (Crab, Vela, Millisecond) to jump to a real pulsar with its true period, surface magnetic field, and age class shown in the quiet corner chips. The folded pulse profile at the foot of the frame fills in over successive rotations and flashes its spike each time a beam crosses the Earth marker's line of sight.

modules.title: Element Fingerprints
modules.blurb: An interactive atomic emission spectroscopy viewer for SSAI. It plots real NIST visible emission lines for six elements onto a 380–700 nm spectrum strip, each line colored by its true wavelength-to-RGB value. Users pick a primary element, optionally overlay a second to compare fingerprints, and click any line to read its exact wavelength — illustrating how SSAI's instruments fingerprint elements in stars and atmospheres.
modules.interaction: Click an element button to display its emission fingerprint; the spectrum, line table, and label update with a smooth reveal. Click "Overlay" then a second element to compare two fingerprints stacked on one strip. Click (or tap) any bright emission line to select it — a readout shows the element, wavelength in nm, and visible color band. Hovering a line highlights it and shows a tooltip. A "Clear overlay" control removes the second element. Everything is keyboard reachable and respects reduced-motion.

modules.title: Star types: the OBAFGKM sequence
modules.blurb: Drag a temperature slider to morph a star through the OBAFGKM spectral sequence and watch its blackbody color, size, class, mass, luminosity and place on the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram update in real time.
modules.interaction: Drag the surface-temperature slider (2,800-42,000 K) or tap an O/B/A/F/G/K/M class chip; the central star recolors and resizes by blackbody physics while the HUD and the dot on the H-R strip track live.

modules.title: Exoplanet Transit Photometry
modules.blurb: An interactive photometry instrument that demonstrates the transit method of exoplanet detection using five real, confirmed exoplanets and their archival parameters. The top panel animates the host star with the selected planet crossing its disk; the bottom panel plots the resulting light curve in real time, showing the characteristic U-shaped dip with correct ingress/egress and the real transit depth. Selecting a planet retunes star size/color, orbital period, and dip depth. Built for a 48-year NASA science company (SSAI) to communicate detection science with the credibility of a real mission-grade tool.
modules.interaction: Click any planet tab to load its real parameters — the star disk resizes and recolors, the orbiting planet rescales, and the light curve below redraws with that planet's true transit depth and period. The simulation runs continuously: watch the planet sweep across the stellar disk while a tracing dot rides the live flux curve and dips exactly when the planet occults the star. A live readout shows period, transit depth, planet radius, and current normalized flux. Play/Pause toggles the animation. Respects reduced-motion (renders a static representative frame) and pauses when scrolled off-screen.

modules.title: Redshift
modules.blurb: Cinematic, full-bleed visualization of cosmological redshift sweeping real galaxy absorption lines across a live spectrum as a function of z, with real SDSS example galaxies, recession velocity, and look-back time.
modules.interaction: Drag the z slider (or use the example-galaxy chips) to redshift the whole spectrum redward in real time; rest-frame ghost lines stay fixed while the observed lines stretch, and the galaxy swatch reddens with z.

modules.title: Gravitational Lens
modules.blurb: Shows how a black hole's gravity bends background starlight into an Einstein ring around a dark photon-sphere shadow.
modules.interaction: Drag the shadow anywhere across the starfield to re-lens stars in real time; adjust mass to grow the horizon and ring, toggle the accretion disk, or recenter.

modules.title: Orbital Mechanics
modules.blurb: A grounded gravitational instrument that opens with a star and three planets already tracing accurate Keplerian ellipses with luminous trails. Tuning a single planet's orbital velocity lets the observer watch the same conservation laws that govern real systems reshape an orbit — circular, to ellipse, to unbound escape — while a quiet readout reports the live total mechanical energy of the system.
modules.interaction: The system runs in graceful motion on load. Use the VELOCITY slider to scale the highlighted planet's speed: below ~1.0 the orbit tightens into an ellipse with the star at one focus; near 1.0 it is circular; above ~1.41x the planet exceeds escape velocity and the trail opens into an unbound hyperbola. The SPEED slider sets the time step for observation. Click empty space to release an additional body on a tangential orbit and watch it settle into the gravitational field. The "Reset system" chip restores the original three-planet configuration. Corner chips report the number of bodies and the system's total energy (negative = bound).

modules.title: Gravitational Waves — The Chirp
modules.blurb: Visualizes the inspiral, merger, and ringdown of two compact objects as ripples in spacetime, with the characteristic strain "chirp" waveform drawing in sync. Auto-loops through the full coalescence with live readouts of component masses, GW frequency, separation, and peak strain.
modules.interaction: Fully autonomous, alive on load. A continuous loop cycles inspiral -> merger -> bright flash -> ringdown -> reset. The single overlaid control toggles the spacetime warp grid on/off. HUD chips in the corners track component masses, gravitational-wave frequency, orbital separation, and peak strain. The strain waveform draws across the lower band in lockstep with the orbit.

modules.title: The SSAI Lens — Sun · Earth · Universe
modules.blurb: A brand-identity centrepiece for SSAI's Instrument Library that frames the company's three scientific domains — Heliophysics, Earth Science, and Astrophysics — as three real NASA images you switch between with elegant cinematic cross-fades. Each lens presents the genuine imagery full-bleed with slow, restrained motion, the domain name, and a single line describing the science SSAI advances there.
modules.interaction: Three quiet segmented tabs (Sun / Earth / Universe) cross-fade between full-bleed real NASA images. The active lens drifts and slowly zooms (a gentle Ken Burns parallax); the domain title and descriptor line settle into place on each switch. A small corner chip names the active science domain. Tabs respond to click, keyboard (arrow keys / Enter), and there is no clutter — the imagery leads.

modules.title: Mission Constellation
modules.blurb: Explore SSAI and NASA's real mission portfolio organized into glowing domain constellations — Sun, Earth, and Universe — on a living starfield. Filter pills re-cluster the field and highlight the active domain; selecting any mission reveals its launch year, domain and purpose.
modules.interaction: Missions appear as labelled glowing nodes grouped into three domain clusters linked by faint constellation lines, drifting gently at rest. Tap a filter pill (All / Sun / Earth / Universe) to smoothly re-cluster the field and spotlight one domain. Click or tap any node to select it and read its details in the lower HUD card.

modules.title: Mission Timeline
modules.blurb: A cinematic horizontal timeline tracing nearly five decades of real NASA Earth- and space-science missions SSAI has supported — from the Voyager launches of 1977 through PACE in 2024 — as a luminous era track of glowing milestone nodes. Visitors drag a scrubber along the track; nearby missions swell with a fisheye proximity effect while the focused one resolves into a large editorial readout with its year, name, and a one-line description. It reframes a sparse list of dates into a living arc of institutional legacy.
modules.interaction: Drag the glowing scrubber handle (or click/tap anywhere on the track) to glide focus along the timeline; the nearest mission node scales up via a fisheye falloff and its details animate into the large readout. Left/right arrow keys step between missions. At rest the focus drifts slowly and autonomously across the eras so the piece is always alive, and a faint starfield with parallax drift keeps the frame full. Use the era toggle to switch the era band labels on or off.

modules.title: Signal Pipeline
modules.blurb: Visualizes SSAI's end-to-end value chain — Observe, Data, Model, Knowledge — as one continuous luminous stream that transforms raw photons into a single crisp insight. Used to communicate "From observation to understanding" with cinematic clarity.
modules.interaction: Alive on load: particles flow left to right and visibly change state at each of four glowing stations (scattered photons collapse into structured data points, fit to a model curve, then converge to one insight). Hovering or tapping a station gently focuses it and lifts its live metric. A single Flow Rate control overlays the speed of the stream; readout chips report throughput and signal yield. Everything self-heals to the canvas size.

modules.title: Animated stat counters
modules.blurb: A row of four headline metrics that count up smoothly the first time the panel scrolls into view, anchoring SSAI's track record on a landing page or about section.
modules.interaction: Scroll the panel into view to trigger a one-time eased count-up (via IntersectionObserver); a faint sweep line crosses each stat as it settles. Reduced-motion shows final values instantly.
